summary: Superb soundboard capture of a rare 1979 Morning Dew with crisp mix and a high‑energy second set.
review: The recording is a clean SBD source that delivers clear, punchy guitars and a booming Phil bass, though a few early‑set verses are missing and there’s some digi‑noise. Musically the show shines thanks to the only 1979 performance of Morning Dew – delivered with a reggae lilt, fiery vocals and an unforgettable transition from NFA. The set also features solid China Cat Sunflower > I Know You Rider and a mixed‑feel Scarlet Begonias > Fire on the Mountain.

ai_rating.rationale: Over two dozen reviews, most 4–5 stars, praise the rarity and power of the Dew and cite excellent soundboard quality; a few minor criticisms lower the average only slightly.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Crisp, balanced mix with strong guitar and bass presence. Minor issues: missing first verses of Minglewood Blues/It's All Over Now, occasional digi‑noise at crowd applause, and a brief tape glitch on Scarlet.

standout_songs: China Cat Sunflower > I Know You Rider
Scarlet Begonias > Fire on the Mountain
poor_songs: Scarlet Begonias (some reviewers found it weak)
show_quality.setlist_flow: First set described as slower and a bit spotty, but the second set bursts with intensity – NFA into a thunderous Morning Dew, highlighted by tight transitions and an unusual reggae‑tinged ending.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Praised for passionate vocals, fiery guitar work, reggae lilt on Dew, and the memorable "I guess it doesn't matter" ad‑libs.
band_member_comments.Phil: Repeatedly called “bombastic” with a booming presence; many note his bass as the driving force of the show.
band_member_comments.Keys: Brent’s early B3 work on the Dew noted as solid and later praised for strong contributions throughout the set.
band_member_comments.Drums: Mickey’s anvil effect highlighted; drums/space segment described as a controlled‑chaos transition into Dew.
